{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken [[:en:Granite|Granite]] stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]]; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as gilt bronze earrings and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yoshinoguchi Station|Yoshinoguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55537636]] Wikidata name: Midoro Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Midoro Kofun | native_name =水泥古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Midoro Kita Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Midoro Kita Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Midoro Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E|region:JP-29|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|circular kofun|WD=Q11394747}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken [[:en:Granite|Granite]] stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]]; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as gilt bronze earrings and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yoshinoguchi Station|Yoshinoguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken [[:en:Granite|Granite]] stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]]; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as gilt bronze earrings and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yoshinoguchi Station|Yoshinoguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Gose, Nara|Gose, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]]. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken [[:en:Granite|Granite]] stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]]; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] such as gilt bronze earrings and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yoshinoguchi Station|Yoshinoguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from [[:en:Yoshinoguchi Station|Yoshinoguchi Station]] on the [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu Railway]] [[:en:Yoshino Line|Yoshino Line]]. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Midoro Kofun | native_name =水泥古墳 | map_caption = Midoro Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E|region:JP-29|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|circular kofun|WD=Q11394747}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|WD=Q41177|lt=Granite}} stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}}; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} such as gilt bronze earrings and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|WD=Q41177|lt=Granite}} stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}}; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} such as gilt bronze earrings and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Midoro Kofun | native_name =水泥古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Midoro Kita Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Midoro Kita Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Midoro Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E|region:JP-29|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|circular kofun|WD=Q11394747}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin}}. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|WD=Q41177|lt=Granite}} stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff}}; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} such as gilt bronze earrings and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station|1=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Midoro Kofun | native_name =水泥古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Midoro Kita Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Midoro Kita Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Midoro Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E|region:JP-29|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|circular kofun|WD=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|WD=Q41177|lt=Granite|1=Granite}} stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}}; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} such as gilt bronze earrings and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station|1=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>

The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.

Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station|1=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Midoro Kofun]][[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Midoro Kofun]][[ja:水泥古墳]]
{{Short description|Kofun period keyhole-shaped Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Midoro Kofun | native_name =水泥古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Midoro Kita Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Midoro Kita Kofun | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Midoro Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E|region:JP-29|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|circular kofun|WD=Q11394747|1=Round Kofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|24|42.45|N|135|44|33.5|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|''' Midoro Kofun'''|水泥古墳|}} is a pair of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Furuse neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Gose, Nara|en|Gose, Nara|WD=Q847334|lt=Gose, Nara|1=Gose, Nara}} in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The pair of tumuli were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1961.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/172705|title=水泥古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref> The tumulus is also called the {{nihongo|'''Imaki-no-sō haka'''|今木の双墓|}}.
==Overview== Midoro Kofun stand side-by-side, about 60 meters apart, on the eastern foot of the Kose mountain range near the Yoshino River basin in the southwestern edge of the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Nara Basin|1=Nara Basin}}. The Koseyama Tomb Cluster, a large group of tombs, is distributed in the northern hills, but the Midoro Kofun not regarded as part of that cluster. The two tumuli are called the "Kita Kofun" and the "Minami Kofun", respectively, and both {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}-style circular mounds, with horizontal-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s, with the Kita Kofun being slightly larger in size.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The Minami Kofun has a diameter of about 25 meters and a height of about six meters, and is orientated to the south. The burial chamber and the antechamber were built with piled up broken {{ill|Granite|en|Granite|WD=Q41177|lt=Granite|1=Granite}} stones, the burial chamber is about 4.6 meters long, 2 meters wide and is currently about 2.2 meters high, the antechamber is about 6.2 meters long, 1.5 meters wide and currently about 1.1 meters high, and contains two stone sarcophagi inside. Both are made of {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|WD=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}}; the body is carved out and the lid is roof-shaped with six rope hanging protrusions. One is located in the main burial chamber, and one on the entrance passage. The one in the main burial chamber is missing its lid, possibly due to grave robbery. The one in the entrance passage is notable for having a lotus motif carved into the front and back rope handing protrusions, and is known as one of the few tombs that show the combination of Kofun culture and Buddhist culture. The floor of the burial chamber is covered with round gravel, and there is a drainage ditch under the gravel bed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} such as gilt bronze earrings and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery have been found.
The Kita Kofun has a diameter of 20 meters and a height of about seven meters, and is also orientated to the south. It consists of a burial chamber and antechamber; the burial chamber is about 5.6 meters long, 2.95 meters wide and 3.4 meters high, the antechamber is about 7.8 meters long, 1.95 meters wide and 1.85 meters high, and is built of huge stones. Approximately 20 clay pipes made of tiles were laid under the floor of the burial chamber as drainage pipes at the time of the burial. The chamber did not contain a stone sarcophagus, but fragments of tuff mount on the floor indicate that such a sarcophagus once existed, and that the tomb had been robbed.<ref name="Shiseki"/> It is currently located on private property and cannot be approached.
The construction date is estimated to be from the late 6th century to the end of the late Kofun period (Kita Kofun) and the early 7th century at the end of the Kofun period (Minami Kofun). Although the identity of the deceased is unknown, these are is believed to be the tombs of a powerful chieftains who introduced Buddhism to the area at an early stage, most probably leaders of the Kose clan, an ancient clan that ruled the area. There has long been a theory that this is the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}} er a description that appears in the December entry for the first year of the reign of [[:en:Empress Kogyoku|Empress Kogyoku]] (642) in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''; However, archaeological evidence indicates that the tumuli pre-date the deaths of Soga no Emishi and Iruka by at least 20 years.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about a 15-minute walk from {{ill|Yoshinoguchi Station|en|Yoshinoguchi Station|WD=Q3615447|lt=Yoshinoguchi Station|1=Yoshinoguchi Station}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|WD=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu Railway|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|WD=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Midoro Minami Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Minami Kofun Midoro Minami Kofun, sendou.jpg|Inside the Minami Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, entrance.jpg|Kita Kofun Midoro Kita Kofun, genshitsu-1.jpg|Inside the Kita Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}}
